content:
So I was reading Lina's thread, and it's a very enlightening discussion on the use of unsafe in kernel Rust. Barring a lot of the GPU stuff I don't understand, I feel there are maybe 4 layers to this discussion:

1) the most basic: what should be considered unsafe? Normal Rust is in user space, and built around its standard library, so it has a pretty well defined notion of safety. whereas kernel Rust interfaces with the rest of the Linux kernel, and more over, with hardware. i can imagine each driver could have slightly different definition of safety. for example, (please forgive my ignorance about GPUs), say dangling pointer to GPU memory could cause graphic operation to fail, but can't touch main memory; gpu drivers might still want to consider that unsafe and try to prevent that.

2) how to design invariant so that it can be compartmentalized. Lina made a point that the gpu driver upholds safety as a whole, even if when you take it apart each part isn't necessarily safe. the example being, IIUC, IoPageTable is safe, but becomes "unsafe" when you put CPU pointers into GPU-mapped memory?

in normal rust, the unsafe boundary is pushed to as late as possible. things are generally considered safe unless it actually cause stuff to go boom on the spot. one relevant example is, converting references to pointers, and pointer arithmetics, are safe. only when you try to dereference a raw pointer, you need to use unsafe. I wonder if the same principle applies here? also, sometimes runtime checks can help you establish invariants that are difficult to do with type system alone.

in general, designing the invariant that has to be upheld by safe interfaces can be difficult, sometimes it's indeed impossible to break thing down.

3) can something be considered safe without perfect knowledge of the hardware? i think it's ok to settle for "it's safe to the best of my knowledge". it's the model seL4 operates on: it's mathematically proven to be safe if its model of X86 matches real hardware. if this is good enough for seL4, it's probably good enough for us? :-)

4) ignore all above points, it just is unsafe, and it's used everywhere. do we then plaster the code with unsafe and safety comments? i am inclined to think it's worthwhile, but i am not sure.

thoughts?
I think Linus' point about "being realistic about the safety guarantees" plays into the first point as well

I think a lot of it boils down to the fact that dealing with hardware is intrinsically unsafe. A bus-mastering device can violate any of the guarantee without IOMMU (which isn't guranteed to be used and is also not fine-grained enough even if its' used).
I think it's fair if we limit ourselves to "we guarantee it's safe as long as the hardware don't misbehave". and then we could probably do some validation on what pointer is passed to the hardware?

I think the crucial point from my thread is that safety/soundness is a property of interfaces. So it is something that can be more clearly defined for the kernel crate, since that is a bunch of interfaces for modules to use. But within any given piece of code, as soon as you introduce one unsafe block, that can impose requirements on other safe code. That's already the case in normal Rust. For example, I was reading the Pin docs earlier today here:

The struct must only be Unpin if all the structural fields are Unpin. This is the default, but Unpin is a safe trait, so as the author of the struct it is your responsibility not to add something like impl<T> Unpin for Struct<T>. (Notice that adding a projection operation requires unsafe code, so the fact that Unpin is a safe trait does not break the principle that you only have to worry about any of this if you use unsafe.)

Having unsafe in one place means doing something safe in another place can turn the design unsound, and make UB possible.

So in the kernel, it's reasonable to say that interfacing with hardware is unsafe, because we can't know what hardware will do. That's already the case (IoMem is unsafe to instantiate). But we don't have to make every little operation unsafe, since once the programmer uses unsafe once to get at a hardware interface, it is their responsibility that the rest of the code is using it safely. There's no need to plaster unsafe everywhere, you only need to make sure the user has to use unsafe at least once.

That doesn't mean we should fully minimize the number of APIs that are unsafe. For example, even if the design of an abstraction guarantees that the user already used unsafe to instantiate it, there can be some operations that are "extra unsafe" for some reason. Like, something that's more likely to be a footgun for users, or which imposes extra safety requirements above those imposed by the initial use of unsafe. And in those cases, it makes sense to have that API be unsafe too.

Once you get into driver design, the challenge is to compartmentalize things as much as possible and make safety something that you can evaluate for small pieces of code. But for something like a GPU driver, the interactions are complex enough that there are safety aspects that span the whole driver. And in that case, I think the goal should be to make those safety aspects a property of the overall driver design, in a way that it's possible to evaluate at a high level and doesn't mean you need to audit all the code in detail and guarantee it's correct. So, safety is tiered: some things (especially those tied to individual unsafe blocks) enforce safety locally and are easy to audit at a low level, and some things are high-level concepts where you can audit safety by just understanding the design, without digging into the details of the implementation too much.

In the end, I think we can agree that the goal should be to minimize bugs. You have to use unsafe to write drivers, so there is no way to make any hard guarantees like there is with userspace Rust. But Rust's tools let us greatly reduce the surface where bugs appear, if used properly, and that's what makes it an awesome language for drivers too. It's all about the balance between ergonomics and "scary markers" (like unsafe), so that us developers can comfortably write good code!

Just to give some examples from my driver:

This is an example of typical Rust unsafe usage. I loan out references-as-pointers to items within an array together with guards, and the design of the abstraction here is sound as explained in the comment. It's just working around Rust's lifetime model to do something special, but which you can reasonably see is ultimately sound.

This unsafe function just adds an offset to a GPU pointer. The safety model is different from Rust's pointers/references: you can think of these GPU pointers as being somewhere between those two. They are expected to be valid and GpuPointer enforces lifetimes (possibly in a leaky way, but it tries). I don't expect adding random offsets to pointers to be a thing I actually do normally, so this is unsafe to represent that you really need to know what you're doing if you use it. And then this macro uses that method to make inner pointers to fields in GPU objects, and that is safe since you know the offset is within the object the original pointer pointed to. This is an example of using the unsafe marker for something that isn't directly about CPU-side safety, but which is analogous, and then having a safe abstraction on top.

And this is an example of GPU-side safety enforced by design. The work queues have (weak, so unchecked) GPU pointers to the GPU context and notifier list, and those need to outlive the work queue for things to be sound. But those objects are owned by the Renderer that owns the work queues, and come after, and fields are dropped in declaration order, so that is enforced by the design: the work queues will be dropped first, so there can be no dangling pointers, and you don't have to dig into the details of the rest of the code to understand that. Obviously this is a "weaker" guarantee (the rest of the code could replace those weak pointers, cache them, or do something silly like that), but the point is that that's not something you'd do without a good reason, so it's not a mistake that would happen by accident. In this case, just using GpuWeakPointer implies the programmer is responsible for the lifetime management here, so I don't think adding unsafe markers would make sense. GpuWeakPointer is, within this driver, itself a (lack of) safety indication, and it makes more sense to just document that.

I think the first two examples are great in showing that it often is possible to confine unsafe to a single module. I think that this will be perfectly fine for the kernel even in the longer term. We could advise/require driver writers to add a # Safety chapter to the module documentation if their module uses unsafe and document what general invariants they intend to uphold in the module.
That way it will be clear for reviewers and future authors what they have to be careful about.

Asahi Lina said:

And this is an example of GPU-side safety enforced by design. The work queues have (weak, so unchecked) GPU pointers to the GPU context and notifier list, and those need to outlive the work queue for things to be sound. But those objects are owned by the Renderer that owns the work queues, and come after, and fields are dropped in declaration order, so that is enforced by the design: the work queues will be dropped first, so there can be no dangling pointers, and you don't have to dig into the details of the rest of the code to understand that. Obviously this is a "weaker" guarantee (the rest of the code could replace those weak pointers, cache them, or do something silly like that), but the point is that that's not something you'd do without a good reason, so it's not a mistake that would happen by accident. In this case, just using GpuWeakPointer implies the programmer is responsible for the lifetime management here, so I don't think adding unsafe markers would make sense. GpuWeakPointer is, within this driver, itself a (lack of) safety indication, and it makes more sense to just document that.

I would argue that making WorkQueue::new unsafe would be a good idea. It has the additional safety requirement of "these pointers live at least as long as I do". It also does not plaster unsafe over everything, because it only sits at the initialization (the thing about the drop order might not be obvious to most kernel reviewers, so I think if there were a comment explaining that, it would be good).
This also has precedent in the kernel crate, before LockClassKey was introduced as a safe wrapper for *mut bindings::lock_class_key, all initialization functions for locks were unsafe. The requirement was that the lockclasskey lived at least as long as the lock. On the rust side this was almost always 'static which is why it is now tracked by lifetimes.

@Asahi Lina

But within any given piece of code, as soon as you introduce one unsafe block, that can impose requirements on other safe code.

I think in normal Rust it's the other way around. It's safe code imposes requirements on unsafe blocks. i.e. there is some "safety contract" that every piece of unsafe code must uphold. for example, simple things like "not creating 2 &mut to the same object". And as long as your unsafe blocks play to this rule, you don't need to worry about them in the back of your mind all the time. in normal Rust at least.

And if your function is not guaranteed to uphold this contract, then the whole function needs to be unsafe.
There certainly is the problem that the "safety contract" isn't yet fully defined for normal Rust (to the best of my knowledge). But I think this is generally how unsafe code should operate

In the end, I think we can agree that the goal should be to minimize bugs.

Yes, fully agree with you. Don't let perfect be the enemy of good

However:

You have to use unsafe to write drivers, so there is no way to make any hard guarantees like there is with userspace Rust.

There might as well be no way to make the same level of guarantees, but the necessity of unsafe can't be one of the reasons. Userspace Rust has to use unsafe too! The point is that the unsafe-ty is contained, and documented with SAFETY: comments.

unsafe is when you need to do something that's beyond the language's ability to prove safe - you know after the unsafe {} block is done all the safety contracts and invariants still hold, but the compiler couldn't see that fact with its type system and borrow checker.

You are just telling the compiler "trust me with this" with unsafe.

Or at least that's the philosophy of normal Rust. If the kernel is a land so full of danger we can't abstract a reasonable safety contract/invariant (how to design such an invariant? is my point (2)), only then we enter the discussion whether we should forgo the use of the unsafe keyword, because if we use it as Rust intended we will be using it everywhere (my point (4))

yshui said:

Asahi Lina

But within any given piece of code, as soon as you introduce one unsafe block, that can impose requirements on other safe code.

I think in normal Rust it's the other way around. It's safe code imposes requirements on unsafe blocks. i.e. there is some "safety contract" that every piece of unsafe code must uphold. for example, simple things like "not creating 2 &mut to the same object". And as long as your unsafe blocks play to this rule, you don't need to worry about them in the back of your mind all the time. in normal Rust at least.

And if your function is not guaranteed to uphold this contract, then the whole function needs to be unsafe.

I think you're conflating two concepts here. There are some things that are UB or can lead to UB in unsafe code, and which unsafe code must not do. But unsafe code imposes requirements on safe code all the time (just look at many of the Safety comments we already have, many of them rely on invariants upheld by safe code).

yshui said:

unsafe is when you need to do something that's beyond the language's ability to prove safe - you know after the unsafe {} block is done all the safety contracts and invariants still hold, but the compiler couldn't see that fact with its type system and borrow checker.

But that knowledge relies on knowledge about things outside the unsafe block very often! So unsafe code isn't necessarily self-contained, it very often relies on facts about its safe environment in order to not invoke UB. This is the case in normal Rust, and it's the case in the kernel. Just look at the Pin documentation I linked - that's a document telling you you need to uphold certain rules when implementing a safe trait, but those rules only come into play if there is unsafe code involved elsewhere. If you have that unsafe code, and then don't follow the rules in the safe bit, your code is unsound and can invoke UB, just by changing safe code.

Put another way: if your code has zero unsafe blocks, then you can make arbitrary changes and it will remain safe. If your code has one unsafe code, that guarantee is instantly lost. The unsafe code might be sound, it might invoke UB, or it might be sound only if the rest of the code does certain things a certain way. This is, and has always been, how unsafe Rust works. The standard library is full of examples of this. You cannot safely make arbitrary changes to arbitrary safe code within a module that uses unsafe or uses modules whose API does not promise soundness.

And then you can define a soundness API boundary such that you can say "okay, if you don't touch any of the code in this module, then even though it contains unsafe blocks, it is sound no matter what the user does outside of it". That's how you get the soundness/safety guarantees back.

I get the feeling there's a lot of confusion around safety in Rust... unsafe blocks don't mark which parts of the code are unsafe and you have to audit to ensure soundness, they mark the entry point of that analysis. That analysis can, and very often does, then extend outside those unsafe blocks. And the goal is to have that analysis terminate (to know that, exhaustively, all possible code paths are sound) within a reasonable scope so that we can know that the code is correct. But that is very often going to involve safe code too.

@Asahi Lina

I think you're conflating two concepts here.

I think we might be talking over each other a little bit, or maybe you could have been confused by "SAFETY" comments on top of unsafe fn and unsafe impls? Those unsafe imposes requirements on other code, regarding how they can be used. But those can't be used from safe code anyway.

The other use of unsafe is unsafe { ... }. Those shouldn't impose extra requirements on other code if used as intended.

Let me just pull up a random example, Guard::new is an unsafe fn, because if the caller doesn't own the lock bad things happen. So yes, it imposes requirements on other code. It's called here, in the unsafe {...}, with a safety comment justifying its use. Other safe code does not have to be aware of the existence of this unsafe block, because it's behind SpinLock::lock, which is safe. i.e. it doesn't impose requirements on safe code.
If an unsafe block imposes requirements on safe code, then its unsafe-ness is "leaking"

But that knowledge relies on knowledge about things outside the unsafe block very often! So unsafe code isn't necessarily self-contained, it very often relies on facts about its safe environment in order to not invoke UB.

Hmm, I think I see where you are coming from. I see it as the safe code establishing an invariant that any unsafe code can rely on. When I say unsafe imposes something on other code, I think about requirements unique to each unsafe block. Like the safety comments on unsafe fns, usually each unsafe fn has its own unique requirements.

Whereas in Pin's case, the safe code established an invariant that any unsafe block can rely on. So it's less each unsafe block is imposing its own requirement.

This also is my point (2) which is about how we could design an invariant like this.
Reading the safety comment on IoMem::try_new. To me it's odd that IoMem::try_new is unsafe. Shouldn't initiating DMA be unsafe instead? Maybe I am missing some context here.

@Asahi Lina

Put another way: if your code has zero unsafe blocks, then you can make arbitrary changes and it will remain safe. If your code has one unsafe code, that guarantee is instantly lost. The unsafe code might be sound, it might invoke UB, or it might be sound only if the rest of the code does certain things a certain way.

OK, I think I understand your perspective a bit better now. Yes, it is true that some pre-condition must be setup before entering an unsafe block. But I don't think in practice it's as terrifying as you made it out to be.

Usually code that sets up the pre-condition is contained within the function the unsafe block resides, so you can touch everything else without worrying about it. In some cases it would span 2, 3 structs, bound by a contract/invariant between them. Unpin is the exceptional case where there is a language wide invariant, but that isn't usually problematic since there is unlikely to be a safe, generic fn that does pin projections.

However, my bigger point is, there should be no unsafe-ness leftover. i.e. after an unsafe block has done executing, safe code doesn't have to be aware that an unsafe block has ever been executed. After an unsafe block, safe code should be able to do whatever it wants and not cause UB.
Could you then help review Asahi Lina's GPU driver code to prove your point? :smiley:
I should have understood Lina's points better before I replied. I apologize.

In this case, just using GpuWeakPointer implies the programmer is responsible for the lifetime management here, so I don't think adding unsafe markers would make sense.

Sounds like these WeakPointers are analogous to raw pointers, which also doesn't have lifetime checks. I think it's worthwhile to make accessing these pointers be unsafe. work queue can encapsulate these behind a safe interface, other code that uses weak pointers have to use unsafe {} to indicate they are doing it under their own risk.

yshui said:

Other safe code does not have to be aware of the existence of this unsafe block, because it's behind SpinLock::lock, which is safe. i.e. it doesn't impose requirements on safe code.

But it does, it's right there! The unsafe code is sound because the spinlock was just acquired, in safe code. Had that not been the case the unsafe code would be a problem. In this case it happens to be two lines of code before the unsafe block, but it can be much further away, such as the Pin example I mentioned where you are no allowed to impl Unpin without following certain rules if you have any projection operations (which contain the unsafe {} part).
@Asahi Lina yeah, you are right. I addressed that in a comment below

yshui said:

However, my bigger point is, there should be no unsafe-ness leftover. i.e. after an unsafe block has done executing, safe code doesn't have to be aware that an unsafe block has ever been executed. After an unsafe block, safe code should be able to do whatever it wants and not cause UB.

Okay, that's an interesting point: that the unsafe code can impose preconditions on safe code but shouldn't impose postconditions on safe code. Is that actually documented somewhere as a desirable design in Rust? I can see how it makes some sense, but it also seems somewhat academic in other ways, depending on the exact design.

To me it sounds like the more important question is whether those conditions (pre or post) leak, and to what extent, outside module/impl boundaries. Obviously we agree that them leaking across a public API boundary is a bug.
Actually, I can find counterexamples to the "no postconditions" idea, I'm pretty sure. Give me a second.

The information on how to correct write unsafe Rust code is still obscure and sparse. And parts of it is not even defined (e.g. Stacked borrow is still WIP). But I think this is generally the model how unsafe code should be written.

Think of it this way: a library using unsafe code should have no effect on you, if you only write safe code. However this promise can be broken if the library's unsafe {} blocks don't uphold the correct postconditions.
Right, and that's why we agree that public interfaces should be sound.
As for postconditions, consider this. That's unsafe code imposing postconditions on the safe code that follows.

In this example, it's probably better if the unsafe block encapsulate the whole lifetime of this. Because otherwise it would be possible for safe code in that function to exhibit UB. But yeah, this is a good example, and I'm not 100% sure if this breaks the unsafe code guide line.
partially because the guideline isn't complete yet.
I will try to see if I can find a reference.

Soundness is a type system concept (actually originating from the study of logics) and means that the type system is "correct" in the sense that well-typed programs actually have the desired properties. For Rust, this means well-typed programs cannot cause Undefined Behavior. This promise only extends to safe code however; for unsafe code, it is up to the programmer to uphold this contract.

I take this as saying code outside unsafe blocks, as long as they compile, can never cause UB.
source is: https://rust-lang.github.io/unsafe-code-guidelines/glossary.html#soundness-of-code--of-a-library
So yeah, I think that unsafe block is probably incorrect.

y86 said:

I would argue that making WorkQueue::new unsafe would be a good idea. It has the additional safety requirement of "these pointers live at least as long as I do". It also does not plaster unsafe over everything, because it only sits at the initialization (the thing about the drop order might not be obvious to most kernel reviewers, so I think if there were a comment explaining that, it would be good).
This also has precedent in the kernel crate, before LockClassKey was introduced as a safe wrapper for *mut bindings::lock_class_key, all initialization functions for locks were unsafe. The requirement was that the lockclasskey lived at least as long as the lock. On the rust side this was almost always 'static which is why it is now tracked by lifetimes.

Right... the problem here is that you can't actually achieve safety guarantees for firmware operations without plastering unsafe everywhere. Rust is great for CPU-side safety because it encourages writing code that is inherently safe (which it is if you don't use unsafe). But the firmware is written in C, and does unsafe things all over the place, and imposes a huge number of preconditions on the driver. If we actually tried to enumerate all possible ways the driver can crash the firmware and make sure you always need to use unsafe somewhere around those places, we'd end up with half the driver being in unsafe blocks. Sure, making WorkQueue::new unsafe is a minor change, but it's also just one of a million ways you can crash the firmware by not upholding invariants. So I don't think it's actually useful to "just throw some of these in", because it's a battle that we cannot win...

That's why I say I'm using Rust's features to help with the driver development, to make crashing the firmware by accident much harder, and to make those mistakes unlikely. But I don't think I can reasonably contain all the firmware-side unsafety in Rust-side unsafe. It's just not practical. Just the fact that I was able to use Rust's lifetime features to manage GPU-side pointer lifetimes is amazing! And it works, things are stable! But if you ask me to try to apply the same strict rules we apply to CPU-side Rust abstractions... I don't think I can do that.

Put another way: I'm not interested in marking ways I can crash the firmware as unsafe. I'm interested in making them not compile, as much as I can, and hard to do by accident, elsewhere!
Or look at it this way: if safe code breaks precondition, then unsafe code does UB, which is acceptable; if unsafe code breaks postcondition, then safe code does UB, which is not.

yshui said:

Soundness is a type system concept (actually originating from the study of logics) and means that the type system is "correct" in the sense that well-typed programs actually have the desired properties. For Rust, this means well-typed programs cannot cause Undefined Behavior. This promise only extends to safe code however; for unsafe code, it is up to the programmer to uphold this contract.

I take this as saying code outside unsafe blocks, as long as they compile, can never cause UB.

From the next line (emphasis mine):

Accordingly, we say that a library (or an individual function) is sound if it is impossible for safe code to cause Undefined Behavior using its public API.

That's why I keep talking about API boundaries...
Yeah, a library should be sound, but in general Rust code should be sound.

Yes, but there is no hard rule for where the soundness boundary should be for code that isn't a library. It's up to the programmer. Sometimes the design can be improved and that boundary moved closer to the unsafe block, of course.
That's an interesting point. I think you are saying it's OK for a library's code to be potentially unsound, as long as it's public interface is sound?

Soundness is a property of the interface itself. You cannot define soundness (in a useful way in the context of Rust) as an absolute concept. It has to be attached to an interface, because there has to be a boundary where you say "if everything to this side of the boundary is safe, then the code exhibits no UB".

So it doesn't make sense to say "a library's code is unsound". If the public interface is sound, the library is sound. It makes sense to say "this internal interface of the library is sound" and that's a good thing if you can achieve it, because it makes writing a correct library easier!
More accurately, soundness is a property of the implementation viewed through an interface. So the library is sound viewed through its public API.

In the trivial examples of unsafe code we discussed, that code is sound viewed through the function call interface. The risk of UB does not escape the function body. Those are the easy cases, of course ^^

Hmm, the concepts are a bit conflated here. There is the soundness of a type system, which means if a program type checks, it's guaranteed to not have UB. And there is the soundness of a library, which is as you explained.

And of course, Rust's type system requires the programmer to write unsafe code correctly, otherwise a program that type checks can have UB.

What I meant by "the library's code being potentially unsound", is that the library's unsafe code is written in a way, that even if it compiles, its safe code can still possibly cause UB.

The question is "possibly cause UB" under what conditions? If those conditions can be triggered by a user of the library, the library is unsound. If those conditions can only be triggered by changing the library itself, then the library is sound.

In the example you gave, the function interface is sound, and the function most likely does not have UB. But it uses unsafe code in a way that it's possible to cause UB in safe code, which I think is running against the guideline.

But we do that all the time with unsafe code. It's fundamental to how it works in Rust, and it is a good thing, because using unsafe blocks removes a lot of safeguards from rust, and it makes no sense to wrap things in unsafe just because they need to uphold preconditions for other unsafe code. That just increases the amount of other mistakes you can make that the compiler won't catch for you any more.
Because this weakens the compiler's ability to make sure the library's interface is indeed sound as you claimed.
Sorry, that's continuing from my previous message
What's the best way of phrasing this ^^; Let me organize my thoughts a little bit.

I think the crux here is that Rust defines the source of the UB as the unsafe code. So no matter how many preconditions/postconditions it imposes on surrounding code, if there is UB, from the point of view of the Rust language contract, it's the unsafe code's fault. However, that is different from the point of view of the programmer, because if the programmer deliberately chooses to write the unsafe code in a way that imposes those pre/postconditions, they are assuming responsibility for upholding them in the safe code.

Ah, firstly, there is a misuse of terminology, sorry to be pedantic. I was saying unsafe code should upheld postconditions, in other words, they should not deprive later safe code of preconditions that should hold.

It's somewhat wrong to say they impose postconditions, I think.

I think this all comes down to reduce the amount of context you have to keep in mind when you code. Rust says as long as your code compiles, it does not have UB, so you don't need to remember ownership, lifetime, etc. all the time, the compiler checks them for you. However, there is unsafe blocks. If like you said, unsafe can sometimes not upheld their contract - so use the example, that unsafe fails to upheld the contract that there should not be a &mut in safe code to a pinned object. If this happens, you have extra context to keep in mind: I can't move out of this. And if you forget that, the code goes boom.
Granted, in that example, this context only lasts until the end of that function, but the general point stands.
If all your unsafe blocks upheld their contract, then there is no extra mind baggage when you are writing safe code.
So yeah, if I was to review the Rust kernel patch, that unsafe block is definitely something I would point out ^^

yshui said:

So yeah, I think that unsafe block is probably incorrect.

I disagree, unsafe blocks should only encapsulate the operation that is actually unsafe.

Asahi Lina said:

Right... the problem here is that you can't actually achieve safety guarantees for firmware operations without plastering unsafe everywhere. Rust is great for CPU-side safety because it encourages writing code that is inherently safe (which it is if you don't use unsafe). But the firmware is written in C, and does unsafe things all over the place, and imposes a huge number of preconditions on the driver. If we actually tried to enumerate all possible ways the driver can crash the firmware and make sure you always need to use unsafe somewhere around those places, we'd end up with half the driver being in unsafe blocks. Sure, making WorkQueue::new unsafe is a minor change, but it's also just one of a million ways you can crash the firmware by not upholding invariants. So I don't think it's actually useful to "just throw some of these in", because it's a battle that we cannot win...

Now I have become too curious if it would actually be that bad (I believe you, I just want to see if with my own eyes). So when I have more time for this I am definitely going to make a fork...

Asahi Lina said:

Put another way: I'm not interested in marking ways I can crash the firmware as unsafe. I'm interested in making them not compile, as much as I can, and hard to do by accident, elsewhere!

That is excellent design. And I still think that if it is possible to crash the firmware via another way. That way should be marked unsafe.

I disagree, unsafe blocks should only encapsulate the operation that is actually unsafe.

:cry: I have the feeling that people aren't really aligned on what unsafe blocks should do. I think unsafe block should be written in a way that it's impossible to cause UB in safe code.

My argument for this is in the later message

I think it should mark unsafe operations and be accompanied with a safety comment explaining what invariants are upheld. That way it is easier to spot during review if the unsafe operation was used correctly.
I do not really see a difference between UB happens in safe code (because it violated invariants) vs UB happens in unsafe code.

Well, it's the mindset when writing code, isn't it? In safe code, you don't need to keep in mind which invariants you must hold. The unsafe {} block delimits the boundary where you need to worry vs where you don't.
Take a look at Vec. You constantly need to be aware that modifying len might result in UB later, because you set it to the wrong value (We currently do not have unsafe fields).
Is there a specific example? Vec is pretty long.
BTW do you know if there is any consensus on exactly what unsafe can and can't do? I only know about the UCG working group, which seems to be still WIP

yshui said:

Is there a specific example? Vec is pretty long.

I mean as a general concept. The programmer of Vec needs to be aware that they cannot change the length arbitrarily. This is reflected by the scarcity of self.len = and self.len +=.

yshui said:

BTW do you know if there is any consensus on exactly what unsafe can and can't do? I only know about the UCG working group, which seems to be still WIP

My understand of the discussion (or what I learn most from the discussion) is the "style" of unsafe, i.e. pre-condition unsafe vs post-condition unsafe.

Most of the unsafe marking is pre-condition style, that is: unsafe marks the code that may directly invoke UB under some conditions, for example, dereferencing a raw pointer. The advantage of this is you can always check these conditions at unsafe invocations. I guess people all agree that pre-condition unsafe is preferred

Unlike pre-condition, post-condition unsafe is that if you have an invocation of the unsafe API/function, you will need to take care of all the following related (even) safe code as well to guarantee soundness. For example, Pin::new_unchecked: "by calling this method you promise that the reference P dereferences to will not be moved out of again".

Overall, post-condition unsafe should be avoided. And if I understand their points correctly (I'm going to guess what's in their mind, so could be wrong). Miguel Ojeda said:

Now, the main question in this discussion is where that safety boundary should be. For instance, if I understand correctly, Lina is arguing for having the boundary on the whole kernel driver, while typically the boundary is the Rust module.

More specifically, I think the boundary should be as small as practical, but there should be no hard rule that the boundary has to be smaller than the whole kernel driver.

The module boundary makes sense when modules offer self-contained pieces of functionality, like in the standard library. There are no external constraints on that, so they can achieve a sound interface just by being well-designed. But within a driver (particularly complex ones), modules are a means of organizing code. Since the driver is driving one piece of hardware, it's unlikely that that hardware can also be neatly divided into equivalent modules, each of which can be safely driven in a self-contained way. So, it follows that there will most likely be safety requirements that span modules within a driver.

Put another way, I think "keep module APIs sound" should be a rule for the kernel crate and a suggestion/guideline/soft design goal for drivers. However, having driver UAPIs be sound is security-critical, while having kernel APIs be sound is not (it's just a really good idea). That's why it's really important to still strive to contain safety requirements within a driver to manageable blocks, so we can increase the chances of the overall code being correct.

What about something like this? First, for Safety comments in unsafe blocks (typically CPU-side safety relevant), we could have a Soundness boundary line that sets the scope for what code needs to be aware of the invariants it relies on. E.g. Soundness boundary: module (allowing free-form text for more complicated cases). This could be module by default since that is the normal default in Rust, but it could be narrower too (e.g. fn for when those invariants don't escape the current function). Then, for anything that escapes module-level soundness (including hardware-related or MM-related safety issues not usefully documented in individual unsafe blocks, if any), we could have // SOUNDNESS: comments at the function, struct, or module level that document the pre/postconditions that are implied in the design that users need to uphold.

I also thought of an additional wrinkle to this topic: I have unsafe blocks that rely on the correctness (not soundness!) of other modules for soundness. Not in the sense that those modules have to uphold any invariants specific to that one unsafe block specifically, but rather that simply those invariants rely on the documented, intended behavior of other modules (i.e. them being bug-free). I suspect this is not uncommon in Rust too, you can probably find examples in the standard library and third-party crates of this kind of relationship. For example, that first example I linked earlier relies on the SlotAllocator never creating duplicate slots, because duplicate slots would imply mutable pointer aliasing in the unsafe block that relies on that behavior. But SlotAllocator could create those duplicate slots without using unsafe, because slots are instantiated via closure callback and nothing stops it from just making two with the same index. So, on paper, that unsafe block's soundness requirements escape its module, but that's just because it relies on another module not being buggy/malicious in its own implementation (that has nothing to do with that unsafe block on its own). Relying on module dependencies doing their job (especially fairly fundamental/simple ones) seems like a very natural thing to do, so I'd be very surprised if it isn't common in other Rust code (e.g. how much unsafe code would become UB if Vec stopped working, but not in an intrinsically unsafe way? E.g. if it dropped elements instead of pushing them, or did some other random safe thing that breaks its API contract?).

That looks like dynamic to me, but as I think I mentioned, it's just too much work to try to check every field for valid values, and we don't even have a good idea of what the rules are... at the end of the day, on some level, the answer is "the driver is driving firmware safely because what it currently does doesn't crash the firmware". In a way, I think Rust helps remove a lot of the uncertainty/unpredictability, even when the rules aren't encoded in a way the compiler can check them. For example, if the current driver handles object ownership relations (in cases of weak pointers where it isn't explicitly codified) in a certain way with a certain drop order, that drop order is most likely going to stay the same no matter what userspace does in practice, so if it works, it's correct.

It might be worth introducing some cross-checks on firmware state (e.g. checking for completion as in a specific flag in a struct before dropping it), but that comes with its own perils due to cache coherency (a lot of the structs the firmware writes are not cache-coherent and only synced in one directly when the driver first instantiates them, so we can't actually rely on readback).

So in the end we come back to driver design. In the case of jobs, those jobs are owned by the queue after submission, and they only get dropped after the completion notification has arrived, so unless some of that machinery is horribly broken, there's no way for a job to be dropped before it is complete.

Though you do need to be careful with some cases, like the whole queue being dropped (I think I don't handle that properly right now, but we also don't even know how to cancel in-progress jobs, so there's a whole question to be asked there! This doesn't affect the current driver implementation because job submission is synchronous anyway, but will matter once we have the real UAPI.)
